content.text: Reliable data pipelines share two unglamorous but critical properties: you can run them twice without breaking anything, and you can run them on just the new data instead of reprocessing everything. These two ideas—idempotency and incrementality—are what separate a fragile script that needs babysitting from a production pipeline that recovers from failure on its own and scales to billions of rows. The sections below build up both concepts from the ground up, with Python examples throughout.

means that running an operation once produces the same result as running it many times. If a pipeline step writes 1,000 rows, running it again should still leave exactly those 1,000 rows—not 2,000, not duplicates. The word comes from mathematics: an idempotent function
. Applying it repeatedly changes nothing after the first time.

means processing only the data that is new or changed since the last successful run, rather than reprocessing the entire dataset every time. Instead of recomputing yesterday's, last week's, and last year's data on every run, an incremental pipeline tracks a "high-water mark" (a timestamp, an ID, or a partition key) and picks up where it left off.
These two ideas are deeply related but distinct. Incrementality is about
—getting the same answer regardless of retries. In practice you almost always want both, because incremental pipelines retry partial work, and retries are only safe when operations are idempotent.

Data pipelines fail constantly, and not because the code is bad. A network blips, a database connection drops, a machine running the job gets killed by the cluster scheduler, an upstream API rate-limits you halfway through. When any of these happen, the pipeline has to run again. The central question becomes:
what happens when a job that already did half its work runs a second time?

Without idempotency, a retry after partial completion creates corruption: duplicate rows, double-counted revenue, emails sent twice, inventory decremented twice. You end up writing fragile manual cleanup scripts and treating every failure as a fire drill.

Without incrementality, every run reprocesses the full history. A pipeline that took 5 minutes when the dataset was small now takes 6 hours, costs a fortune in compute, and hammers source systems with full-table scans they were never designed to serve repeatedly.
Together, these patterns enable the single most valuable operational property a pipeline can have:
. Orchestrators like Airflow, Dagster, and Prefect are built around the assumption that they can re-run a failed task. That assumption only holds if your tasks are idempotent.
A failed run is just re-run from the start; no manual investigation of "how far did it get" is needed.
Reprocessing a historical date range won't double-count, because writes overwrite rather than append blindly.
Incremental loads touch a tiny fraction of the data, turning hours into seconds and large cloud bills into small ones.
Pulling only changed records reduces load on production databases and APIs.
Idempotent steps can be re-run in isolation during development without polluting downstream tables.
even on top of at-least-once delivery systems (like message queues), which is the realistic foundation for most distributed data systems.
Incremental pipelines must persist a high-water mark or checkpoint somewhere reliable, and that state can itself become corrupted or get out of sync with the data.
Late-arriving and changed data are harder.
If a record arrives with yesterday's timestamp
you've already processed yesterday, naive incremental logic will miss it. Handling this requires lookback windows or change-data-capture.
Idempotent writes are often slower per-row
than blind appends, because they require lookups, merges, or deduplication rather than a simple
Incremental pipelines based on "give me rows changed since X" usually can't see hard deletes in the source, because a deleted row simply vanishes—it doesn't get a new timestamp.
You must choose a natural key, a watermark column, and a write strategy before writing a line of transformation logic.
The most common way to make a write idempotent is to
. There are a few standard techniques:

Before loading a partition (say, one day's data), delete any existing rows for that partition, then insert. Running twice deletes the same partition twice and inserts the same data—net result identical.
: the delete and insert must succeed or fail together. If the delete commits but the insert fails outside a transaction, you've destroyed data.
Each row has a natural unique key. Writing checks whether the key exists; if so it updates, if not it inserts. The same input row written twice lands in the same place.

# PostgreSQL ON CONFLICT upsert
def upsert_users(conn, users):
    conn.executemany(
        """
        INSERT INTO users (user_id, name, updated_at)
        VALUES (%(user_id)s, %(name)s, %(updated_at)s)
        ON CONFLICT (user_id)
        DO UPDATE SET name = EXCLUDED.name,
                      updated_at = EXCLUDED.updated_at
        """,
        users,
    )
Generate a stable, content-derived ID for each record so that the same logical event always produces the same key. Then deduplication is automatic.
If the same event is delivered twice (common with at-least-once queues), both copies hash to the same ID and the upsert collapses them into one row.
(also called a high-water mark or cursor): a value that records how far you've successfully processed.
from durable storage (a metadata table, a file, the orchestrator's state).

def run_incremental(source, sink, state):
    last = state.get_watermark(default="1970-01-01T00:00:00Z")

    new_rows = source.query(
        "SELECT id, updated_at, payload FROM orders "
        "WHERE updated_at > %s ORDER BY updated_at",
        (last,),
    )
    if not new_rows:
        return

    sink.upsert(new_rows)                  # idempotent write
    new_watermark = max(r["updated_at"] for r in new_rows)
    state.set_watermark(new_watermark)     # advance only after success

The ordering in steps 3 and 4 is what makes this safe: if the job crashes after writing but before advancing the watermark, the next run reprocesses the same rows—but because the write is idempotent, reprocessing causes no harm.
A typical idempotent + incremental pipeline has these flowing components:

┌─────────────┐
│   Source    │  (DB, API, files, stream)
│  + cursor   │  exposes a monotonically increasing column
│   column    │  (updated_at, id, sequence number)
└──────┬──────┘
       │ 1. read rows WHERE cursor > watermark
       ▼
┌─────────────┐      ┌──────────────────┐
│  Extract /  │◄─────│  Watermark store │  2. read last position
│  Transform  │─────►│  (metadata table)│  5. write new position
└──────┬──────┘      └──────────────────┘
       │ 3. transformed batch (with stable keys)
       ▼
┌─────────────┐
│    Sink     │  4. idempotent write:
│ (overwrite  │     MERGE / upsert / delete-then-insert
│  or upsert) │     keyed on natural or deterministic ID
└─────────────┘
Three pieces of durable state make this work: the
. The watermark store is typically a small metadata table (
) or, in mature stacks, managed by the orchestrator itself.
A widely used architectural variant is the

model, where data is divided into independent chunks (usually by date). Each partition is processed as an atomic, idempotent unit. This is the backbone of how tools like Airflow think about pipelines: each scheduled interval owns one partition, and re-running an interval just re-overwrites that one partition.
There is almost no production scenario where you want a step that breaks on retry. The cost is modest and the safety benefit is enormous.
The dataset is large enough that full reprocessing is slow or expensive.
The source exposes a reliable cursor (a timestamp or monotonic ID).
Data arrives continuously and you run the pipeline on a schedule (hourly, daily).
You need low end-to-end latency between source change and result.
Prefer full reloads (and skip incrementality) when:
The dataset is small—full reprocessing finishes in seconds anyway. A full reload is simpler and immune to watermark drift and missed late data.
The source has no trustworthy cursor column.
The transformation logic is complex and history-dependent, where reprocessing everything guarantees consistency.
Data quality matters more than speed, and a periodic full rebuild acts as a self-healing reset.
A common mature pattern combines both:
incremental loads daily, with a periodic full reload
(weekly or monthly) to repair any drift from missed updates or deletes.
A practical step-by-step recipe for building an idempotent, incremental Python pipeline:
Find the column(s) that uniquely identify a record (
). If none exists, construct a deterministic hash key.
Find a column that only ever increases for changed rows—usually
. Verify it's actually updated on every change.
Pick upsert (for record-level data with keys) or partition overwrite (for time-bucketed event data).
Create a small, durable table or file to hold the last processed position per pipeline.
Ensure delete+insert or multi-row writes are atomic so a crash leaves the table in a clean state.
Persist the new watermark only after the data write commits.
for late-arriving data—re-scan a small overlapping range (e.g., the last 3 days) on each run. Because writes are idempotent, the overlap causes no duplication.
Test by killing the job mid-run and re-running it; the final state should be identical to a clean run.

from datetime import timedelta

def incremental_with_lookback(source, sink, state, lookback=timedelta(days=3)):
    last = state.get_watermark(default="1970-01-01T00:00:00Z")
    # subtract lookback to catch late-arriving updates
    read_from = max(parse(last) - lookback, MIN_DATE)

    rows = source.query(
        "SELECT * FROM orders WHERE updated_at > %s ORDER BY updated_at",
        (read_from,),
    )
    if not rows:
        return

    sink.upsert(rows)  # safe even though we re-read overlapping rows
    state.set_watermark(max(r["updated_at"] for r in rows))
The difference is what happens on the second run.
Track which files have already been ingested by recording their names.
Re-running this skips already-processed files (incremental) and, even if a file is re-listed, loading it again is harmless if
Consider a daily analytics pipeline at a mid-sized e-commerce company processing roughly 50 million order-events per day into a data warehouse like Snowflake or BigQuery, orchestrated by Airflow.
Each day's data lives in its own
. The Airflow DAG schedules one task instance per day. The task:
Reads orders from the production Postgres replica
, plus a 2-day lookback to capture refunds and status changes posted late.
Transforms and writes to the warehouse using
, so a re-run of June 1st simply re-merges the same rows.
The warehouse table is partitioned by
, so each day is an independent, overwritable unit.

The operational payoff is concrete: when the Postgres replica lagged one night and the task failed at 30% completion, the on-call engineer simply clicked "clear and retry" in Airflow. The task re-ran from scratch, the

overwrote the partial 30% and completed the rest, and the final table was bit-for-bit identical to a clean run—no manual deduplication, no double-counted revenue. A monthly full reload runs on the first of each month to repair any drift from hard deletes that the incremental logic can't detect.
without conflict handling—turns every retry into a duplicate-generating event.
Advancing the watermark before the write succeeds.
If the watermark moves first and the write then fails, the next run skips those rows permanently, causing silent data loss.
on the watermark (or vice versa) carelessly.
re-reads the boundary row every time (wasteful but safe with idempotent writes); using
can skip rows that share the exact boundary timestamp. Decide deliberately, and prefer
Assuming data always arrives in timestamp order. Real systems deliver out of order; without a lookback window, late records are missed forever.
Cursor-based incrementality can't see hard-deleted source rows. The target keeps stale records indefinitely unless you use change-data-capture or periodic full reloads.
Doing delete and insert as separate, uncommitted operations so a crash between them leaves the table empty or partial.
, random values, or unstable ordering inside the transform, so the "same" input produces different output—breaking idempotency even when the write mechanism is correct.
Make every task safe to re-run, then let the orchestrator retry freely.
Design for at-least-once execution; aim for exactly-once
Ensure every record has a stable, unique key—natural if possible, deterministic-hash if not.
, inside or after the same transaction as the data write.
sized to your worst-case late-arrival delay. Idempotency makes the overlap free of side effects.
and treat each partition as an atomic overwritable unit—this composes beautifully with backfills.
, separate from the data itself, and log them on every run for debuggability.
as a self-healing safety net against drift and deletes.
run the task, kill it mid-flight, run it again, and assert the output equals a single clean run.
Incremental design is the primary scaling lever: processing time grows with the
dataset. A pipeline that ingests a steady 50 million rows/day stays roughly constant in runtime even as the historical table grows into the billions, because each run only touches one day.
Idempotent writes do key lookups, which can be slower than blind appends. Mitigate with proper indexes on the merge key and by batching writes rather than row-by-row operations.
must use an index on the cursor column, or it degrades into a full-table scan—defeating the entire purpose.
Partitioning the target on the same column you filter by lets the engine skip irrelevant partitions entirely.
With many parallel partition tasks, a single shared watermark row can become a contention point; per-partition state avoids this.
trade efficiency for completeness—an oversized lookback re-reads large amounts of data each run, so size it to actual late-arrival behavior, not paranoia.
At very high throughput, the same principles extend to streaming (Kafka offsets
watermarks; consumer commits advance them), where idempotent sinks are what make at-least-once delivery produce exactly-once results.
designed for idempotent backfills), Prefect (retries and caching).
macro to filter on a watermark—this is arguably the most widely used implementation of these patterns today.
Snowflake, BigQuery, and Redshift all provide
and native partitioning that make idempotent, partitioned loads straightforward.
Apache Iceberg, Delta Lake, and Apache Hudi add ACID transactions, upserts, and time-travel to data lakes, bringing idempotent writes and incremental reads to file-based storage.
Debezium and similar tools stream inserts/updates/
from source databases, solving the "can't see deletes" limitation of timestamp-cursor incrementality.
SQLAlchemy / psycopg for upserts, pandas/Polars for in-memory transforms, and frameworks like dlt that bundle incremental loading and idempotent writes out of the box.
Needs key + cursor; misses hard deletes

Full reloads are the simplest and self-healing but don't scale. Plain incremental appends are fast but unsafe. The incremental-upsert and partition-overwrite patterns are the practical sweet spot for the vast majority of batch pipelines, while CDC is the answer when you need low latency or must capture deletes.
Wrap related writes in transactions so a failure can never leave a half-written, inconsistent partition. Without atomicity, idempotency claims are an illusion.
It is a single point of failure: corruption or accidental reset can cause silent reprocessing or permanent skipping. Back it up, log every change, and restrict write access.

The most dangerous failure mode is advancing the watermark past unwritten data. Enforce the "write first, advance second" ordering as an inviolable rule, ideally co-committing both in one transaction.
Add row-count and checksum reconciliation between source and target to catch missed or duplicated data that idempotency alone won't reveal (e.g., from a buggy cursor).
A single malformed row shouldn't fail an entire idempotent batch forever on every retry. Route persistently failing records to a dead-letter table so the rest can proceed.
Incremental reads should use read-only credentials scoped to the needed tables, reducing blast radius if the pipeline is compromised.
Idempotency as a defense against duplicate delivery.

Because upstream queues and APIs deliver at-least-once, idempotent writes are not just a convenience—they are the mechanism that prevents accidental double-processing from causing financial or data-integrity damage.

The throughline across every section is a single discipline: make each step produce the same result no matter how many times it runs, and make each run touch only the data it must. A pipeline built on those two rules turns failures from emergencies into automatic, invisible retries.
